core_concept: Fractions: Unit Fractions, Equal Parts, and Placing Fractions on a Number Line
facts: The denominator tells how many equal parts the whole is divided into; a pizza cut into 6 equal slices has denominator 6 whether you eat 1 slice or 5 slices.
The numerator counts how many of those equal-sized parts are selected; 3/4 means you have 3 of the 4 equal pieces.
On a number line from 0 to 1, the fraction 1/3 is exactly one-third of the way from 0 to 1, the same distance no matter what shape or object represents the whole.
Two fractions are equivalent when they mark the same point on the number line; 2/4 and 1/2 are equivalent because both land exactly halfway between 0 and 1.
misconceptions: A larger denominator means a larger fraction — students think 1/8 > 1/4 because 8 > 4, but 1/8 is smaller because dividing the whole into more equal parts makes each part smaller.
Equal parts means the same number of parts, not the same size — students accept unequally-cut slices as a valid fraction, but fractions require all parts to be the same size.
